title: Vatica scaphula
intro: Vatica scaphula, a species of tree in the family Dipterocarpaceae, is a plant native to Southeast Asia, known for its unique scaphula-shaped leaves. The leaves of Vatica scaphula have been traditionally used in folk medicine to treat various health issues, including fever, diarrhea, and respiratory problems, due to its potential anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and antimicrobial properties. The bioactive constituents of Vatica scaphula include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and terpenoids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In traditional herbal medicine, Vatica scaphula is often used to make infusions, decoctions, and tinctures, which are used to treat fever, cough, and gastrointestinal disorders.
taxonomy: Vatica scaphula is a species of tree in the family Dipterocarpaceae. This family is a group of flowering plants commonly known as the "barrel-trees" or "kapoks". The Dipterocarpaceae family is a part of the order Malvales, which includes other flowering plant families like the Malvaceae and Sterculiaceae. The order Malvales is a part of the clade Eurosids II, which is a larger group of flowering plants. The clade Eurosids II is a part of the clade Rosidae, which is a large and diverse group of flowering plants.
uses: Vatica scaphula, also known as Borneo ironwood, has been utilized in traditional medicine for its various health benefits. The plant's roots and bark are often used to treat fever, rheumatism, and other inflammatory conditions. In traditional remedies, Vatica scaphula is also used to aid in digestion and to treat skin conditions such as eczema and dermatitis.
